meta.title: JavaScript & TypeScript
meta.description: JavaScript and TypeScript fundamentals, type safety, and module design

items.text: Does the PR have a clear description of what it does and why?

baseContent.whatItMeans: The PR description should explain the purpose of the change, the problem it solves, and any relevant context a reviewer needs.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Reviewers without context waste time reverse-engineering intent from code. A clear description speeds up review, reduces misunderstandings, and creates a searchable historical record.
baseContent.howToVerify: Read the PR description. Can you understand what changed and why without reading the code? Is there a link to a ticket or design doc?
baseContent.exampleComment: Could you add a bit more context to the PR description? It would help to know why we're changing the retry logic — is this fixing a specific incident?
baseContent.keyTakeaway: A good PR description saves more reviewer time than it costs to write.

items.text: Is the change scoped appropriately, or should it be split into smaller PRs?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Each PR should do one thing. Mixing a bug fix with a refactor with a new feature makes review harder and rollbacks riskier.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Large, multi-concern PRs are slow to review, prone to rubber-stamping, and difficult to revert if one part causes issues. Smaller PRs get faster, higher-quality reviews.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check if the PR touches multiple unrelated areas. Could any part ship independently? Does the diff include both feature work and unrelated cleanup?
baseContent.exampleComment: This PR adds the new endpoint and also refactors the auth middleware. Could we split these into two PRs? The refactor could land first and the endpoint can build on it.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: One concern per PR. If you need a paragraph to explain what it does, it's probably too big.

items.text: Are there any unrelated changes bundled in?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Drive-by fixes, formatting changes, or unrelated tweaks included alongside the main change.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Unrelated changes obscure the actual diff, make git blame less useful, and complicate reverts. They also make the reviewer's job harder by mixing signal with noise.
baseContent.howToVerify: Scan the file list. Are there files that don't seem related to the PR's stated purpose? Are there formatting-only changes in files otherwise untouched?
baseContent.exampleComment: The linting fixes in `utils/format.ts` look unrelated to the auth changes. Mind pulling those into a separate PR so this one stays focused?
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Keep PRs focused. Save drive-by fixes for a dedicated cleanup PR.

items.text: Is dead code removed rather than commented out?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Code that is no longer used should be deleted, not commented out. Version control preserves history if you ever need it back.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Commented-out code creates confusion — is it temporarily disabled? Is it a reference? It adds noise and often never gets cleaned up.
baseContent.howToVerify: Search the diff for large blocks of commented-out code. Check for unused functions, imports, or variables flagged by linters.
baseContent.exampleComment: This commented-out block has been here since the original implementation. Since we have git history, let's remove it to keep things clean.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Delete dead code. Git remembers so you don't have to.

items.text: Are TODO/FIXME comments accompanied by a tracking ticket?

baseContent.whatItMeans: TODO and FIXME comments should reference a ticket number so the work is actually tracked and not forgotten.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Untracked TODOs accumulate as tech debt that nobody owns. With a ticket, the work enters the backlog and can be prioritized.
baseContent.howToVerify: Search the diff for TODO, FIXME, HACK, or XXX comments. Do they include a ticket reference (e.g., JIRA-1234)?
baseContent.exampleComment: This TODO doesn't have a tracking ticket. Could you file one and reference it here? Otherwise it'll likely get forgotten.

codeExamples.label: Bad
codeExamples.code:
// TODO: handle pagination
const results = await fetchAll();

codeExamples.label: Good
codeExamples.code:
// TODO(PROJ-1234): add pagination support
const results = await fetchAll();

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Every TODO should have an owner. A ticket number is that owner.

items.text: Does the commit history tell a coherent story?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Commits should be logically organized with clear messages. Each commit should represent a meaningful, atomic unit of work.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Good commit history helps future developers understand why changes were made, simplifies bisecting bugs, and makes reverts surgical instead of all-or-nothing.
baseContent.howToVerify: Read through the commit log. Are messages descriptive? Are there WIP or fixup commits that should have been squashed? Does the sequence make logical sense?
baseContent.exampleComment: The commit history has several 'WIP' and 'fix' commits. Could you squash these into logical units before merge? It'll make the history much easier to navigate.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Write commits for the person who will read git log in 6 months.

items.text: Are types defined for all function parameters and return values?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Every function should have explicit TypeScript types for its parameters and return value, rather than relying on implicit `any` or type inference for public APIs.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Explicit types catch bugs at compile time, serve as documentation, and make refactoring safer. Without them, TypeScript can't help you when signatures change.
baseContent.howToVerify: Look for functions missing parameter types or return type annotations. Check that exported/public functions have explicit return types.
baseContent.exampleComment: This exported function is missing a return type annotation. Adding one would help consumers understand the contract and catch breaking changes at compile time.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Explicit types on public APIs are documentation that the compiler enforces.

items.text: Are `any` and `unknown` avoided unless absolutely necessary and documented?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Using `any` disables type checking entirely. `unknown` is safer but still requires careful narrowing. Both should be rare and documented.
baseContent.whyItMatters: `any` spreads silently — one `any` can infect an entire call chain, defeating the purpose of TypeScript. It hides real bugs that would otherwise be caught at compile time.
baseContent.howToVerify: Search the diff for `: any` or `as any`. Is each usage justified? Could a more specific type, generic, or `unknown` with narrowing be used instead?
baseContent.exampleComment: This `any` type on the API response means we lose all type safety downstream. Could we type this with the actual response shape, or at least use `unknown` with a type guard?

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Every `any` is a hole in your type safety. Treat it like a code smell that needs justification.

items.text: Are type assertions (`as`) minimized and justified?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Type assertions (`as Type`) tell the compiler 'trust me, I know better.' They bypass type checking and should be used sparingly.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Assertions hide potential runtime errors. If the data doesn't match the asserted type, you get silent bugs instead of compile-time errors.
baseContent.howToVerify: Search for `as ` in the diff (excluding imports). Is each assertion necessary? Could a type guard, generic, or schema validation replace it?
baseContent.exampleComment: This `as UserResponse` assertion on the API response is risky — if the API changes shape, we won't get a compile error. Consider using a runtime validator like Zod here.

codeExamples.label: Bad
codeExamples.code:
const user = JSON.parse(data) as User; // no runtime check
const el = document.getElementById('root') as HTMLDivElement;

codeExamples.label: Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Prefer type guards and runtime validation over type assertions. Assertions are promises the compiler can't verify.

items.text: Are union types and discriminated unions used instead of `any` for flexible data?

baseContent.whatItMeans: When data can be one of several shapes, use union types with a discriminant field instead of `any`. TypeScript can then narrow the type in each branch.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Discriminated unions give you exhaustive checking — the compiler will warn you if you forget to handle a case. `any` gives you nothing.
baseContent.howToVerify: Look for places where `any` is used because data can be multiple shapes. Could a union type with a `type` or `kind` field replace it?
baseContent.exampleComment: This `any` is used because the event can be a click or a keyboard event. A discriminated union (`type: 'click' | type: 'keyboard'`) would give us exhaustive checking here.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Discriminated unions make impossible states unrepresentable and let the compiler check your logic.

items.text: Are `interface` vs `type` used consistently with project conventions?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Projects should pick a convention for when to use `interface` vs `type` and stick with it. Common pattern: `interface` for object shapes, `type` for unions and computed types.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Inconsistency creates confusion. Interfaces support declaration merging and extend; types support unions and mapped types. Using both arbitrarily makes the codebase harder to navigate.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check if the PR mixes `interface` and `type` for similar constructs. Does the project have a convention? Does the new code follow it?
baseContent.exampleComment: The rest of the codebase uses `interface` for object shapes. Could we switch this `type UserProps = { ... }` to `interface UserProps { ... }` for consistency?

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Pick a convention and be consistent. The choice matters less than the consistency.

items.text: Are generic types used where they add value (reusable functions, data structures)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Generics let you write functions and data structures that work with multiple types while preserving type safety. They should be used when a function genuinely works with variable types.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Without generics, you either duplicate code for each type or fall back to `any`. Generics give you reusability with full type safety.
baseContent.howToVerify: Look for functions that accept `any` but could use a generic. Also check that generics aren't overused — a function that only ever takes `string` doesn't need `<T>`.
baseContent.exampleComment: This `fetchData` function returns `any`. Since it's used with different response types, a generic `fetchData<T>(url: string): Promise<T>` would preserve type safety for callers.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Use generics when a function truly works with multiple types. Don't use them when a concrete type suffices.

items.text: Are optional properties (`?`) distinguished from nullable properties (`| null`)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Optional (`name?: string`) means the property may be absent. Nullable (`name: string | null`) means it's always present but may be null. These have different semantics.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Conflating optional and nullable leads to bugs. An optional field missing from an API response is different from a field explicitly set to null (e.g., 'user cleared their bio').
baseContent.howToVerify: Check interface definitions. Does `?` accurately reflect 'may be absent' vs `| null` for 'present but empty'? Are both cases handled in consuming code?
baseContent.exampleComment: Is `bio?: string` correct here? If the API always returns the field but it can be null, `bio: string | null` better represents the contract.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Use `?` for 'may not exist' and `| null` for 'exists but empty.' They mean different things.

items.text: Are `Partial<T>`, `Pick<T>`, `Omit<T>`, `Record<K,V>` used instead of duplicating types?

baseContent.whatItMeans: TypeScript utility types let you derive new types from existing ones instead of manually copying and modifying field lists.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Duplicated types drift apart over time. When the source type changes, derived copies don't update. Utility types keep them in sync automatically.
baseContent.howToVerify: Look for types that duplicate most fields from another type. Could `Partial<T>`, `Pick<T, K>`, `Omit<T, K>`, or `Required<T>` express the same thing?
baseContent.exampleComment: This `UpdateUserInput` type duplicates most fields from `User` but makes them optional. `Partial<Pick<User, 'name' | 'email' | 'bio'>>` would stay in sync automatically.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Derive types from a single source of truth. Utility types keep your type system DRY.

items.text: Are enums used appropriately, or would `as const` objects be more idiomatic?

baseContent.whatItMeans: TypeScript enums have quirks (numeric reverse mapping, bundle size). `as const` objects with derived types are often more idiomatic in modern TS.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Numeric enums generate extra runtime code and can cause surprising behavior. `as const` gives you the same type safety with plain objects that tree-shake better.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check if new enums are numeric or string. Would a `const` object with `typeof` union work better? Does the project have a convention?
baseContent.exampleComment: Consider using `as const` instead of this enum. It produces a smaller bundle and the values are more transparent in debugging since they're just strings.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Prefer `as const` objects over enums in most cases. They're simpler, smaller, and more transparent.

items.text: Are shared types in a central location, not duplicated across files?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Types used across multiple files should live in shared type files or a types directory, not be redefined in each consuming file.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Duplicated types diverge silently. When one copy is updated, others aren't. Centralizing types creates a single source of truth.
baseContent.howToVerify: Search for identical or near-identical type/interface definitions across files. Are shared types imported from a common location?
baseContent.exampleComment: This `User` interface is also defined in `api/types.ts`. Could we import from there instead of redefining it? That way changes propagate automatically.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Define once, import everywhere. Shared types belong in a shared location.

items.text: Is `strict: true` being honored (no `@ts-ignore` without explanation)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Projects with `strict: true` in tsconfig get the full benefit of TypeScript. `@ts-ignore` and `@ts-expect-error` bypass this and should be rare and documented.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Each suppression is a blind spot where bugs can hide. If strict mode is enabled, the team has decided type safety matters — suppressions should be exceptions, not shortcuts.
baseContent.howToVerify: Search the diff for `@ts-ignore` and `@ts-expect-error`. Is each one justified with a comment explaining why? Could the underlying type issue be fixed instead?
baseContent.exampleComment: This `@ts-ignore` suppresses a real type error. Could we fix the underlying issue instead? If not, please add a comment explaining why the suppression is necessary.

codeExamples.label: Bad
codeExamples.code:
// @ts-ignore
const result = someFunction(untypedData);

codeExamples.label: Good
codeExamples.code:
// @ts-expect-error — third-party lib missing types, tracked in PROJ-456
const result = legacyLib.process(data);

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Every type suppression should explain why it exists and ideally link to a ticket to fix it.

items.text: Are custom type guards (`is` return type) used for complex type narrowing?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Custom type guards are functions that return `value is Type`, allowing TypeScript to narrow types in the calling scope based on runtime checks.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Without type guards, you end up with type assertions or repeated `as` casts. Type guards combine runtime safety with compile-time narrowing.
baseContent.howToVerify: Look for places where code checks a value's shape then uses `as` to assert the type. Could a type guard function replace the assertion?
baseContent.exampleComment: This `as AdminUser` assertion after the role check could be replaced with a type guard: `function isAdmin(u: User): u is AdminUser`. That way TypeScript narrows automatically.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Type guards give you runtime validation and compile-time narrowing in one package.

items.text: Are `typeof`, `instanceof`, and `in` checks used correctly for narrowing?

baseContent.whatItMeans: TypeScript narrows types automatically when you use `typeof`, `instanceof`, and `in` checks. These should be used correctly for their intended purpose.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Incorrect narrowing leads to false confidence. `typeof null === 'object'` is a classic gotcha. Using the right check for the right scenario prevents subtle bugs.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check that `typeof` is used for primitives, `instanceof` for class instances, and `in` for checking property existence on objects.
baseContent.exampleComment: Using `typeof` to check for an array won't work (`typeof [] === 'object'`). Use `Array.isArray()` instead for reliable narrowing.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Use the right narrowing check: typeof for primitives, instanceof for classes, Array.isArray for arrays, `in` for property checks.

items.text: Are exhaustiveness checks (`never`) used in switch statements on union types?

baseContent.whatItMeans: When switching on a discriminated union, assigning the default case to `never` ensures the compiler will error if a new variant is added but not handled.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Without exhaustiveness checks, adding a new union member silently falls through. The `never` check turns this into a compile-time error, catching missing cases immediately.
baseContent.howToVerify: Look for switch statements on union types. Does the default case use an exhaustiveness check? Are all variants handled?
baseContent.exampleComment: This switch on `action.type` doesn't handle the new `'reset'` case. Adding an exhaustiveness check in the default would have caught this at compile time.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Exhaustiveness checks turn forgotten switch cases into compile errors. Always use them with discriminated unions.

items.text: Is `const` preferred over `let`, and `let` over `var`?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Use `const` for values that won't be reassigned, `let` for values that will. Never use `var` — it has function scoping and hoisting quirks.
baseContent.whyItMatters: `const` signals intent clearly — this value won't change. `var` has surprising scoping behavior that causes real bugs (e.g., in loops with closures).
baseContent.howToVerify: Search for `var` in the diff. Check if any `let` variables are never reassigned and could be `const`.
baseContent.exampleComment: This `let` is never reassigned. Switching to `const` makes the intent clearer and prevents accidental mutation.

codeExamples.label: Bad
codeExamples.code:
var users = [];
let config = loadConfig(); // never reassigned

codeExamples.label: Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Default to `const`. Use `let` only when reassignment is needed. Never use `var`.

items.text: Are strict equality (`===`, `!==`) used instead of loose equality?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Always use `===` and `!==` instead of `==` and `!=`. Loose equality performs type coercion with surprising results.
baseContent.whyItMatters: `==` coerces types before comparing: `0 == ''` is true, `null == undefined` is true, `'0' == false` is true. These cause subtle, hard-to-find bugs.
baseContent.howToVerify: Search the diff for `==` and `!=` (excluding `===` and `!==`). The only acceptable use is `== null` to check for both null and undefined.
baseContent.exampleComment: This uses `==` for comparison. Please switch to `===` to avoid type coercion surprises. The one exception is `== null` for null/undefined checks.

codeExamples.label: Bad
codeExamples.code:
if (value == '') { ... }
if (count != 0) { ... }

codeExamples.label: Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Always use strict equality. The only exception is `== null` as a null/undefined shorthand.

items.text: Are template literals used instead of string concatenation?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Use template literals (backtick strings with `${expr}`) instead of string concatenation with `+`.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Template literals are more readable, support multi-line strings, and make complex interpolations clearer than chained `+` operators.
baseContent.howToVerify: Search for string concatenation with `+` where template literals would be clearer, especially with multiple variables.
baseContent.exampleComment: This string concatenation is getting hard to read. A template literal would be cleaner: `` `Hello ${user.name}, you have ${count} items` ``

codeExamples.label: Bad
codeExamples.code:
const msg = 'Hello ' + user.name + ', you have ' + count + ' items';
const url = baseUrl + '/api/v1/' + resource + '/' + id;

codeExamples.label: Good
codeExamples.code:
const msg = `Hello ${user.name}, you have ${count} items`;
const url = `${baseUrl}/api/v1/${resource}/${id}`;

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Template literals are cleaner than concatenation for any string with variables.

items.text: Are destructuring and spread operators used appropriately (not overused)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Destructuring extracts values from objects/arrays cleanly. Spread creates copies. Both should be used where they improve clarity, not where they obscure it.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Destructuring reduces repetition (`props.name, props.email` → `{name, email}`). But deeply nested destructuring or spreading too many properties hurts readability.
baseContent.howToVerify: Look for repeated property access that could use destructuring. Also check for overly complex destructuring that would be clearer written out.
baseContent.exampleComment: This function accesses `config.database.host`, `config.database.port`, and `config.database.name` repeatedly. Destructuring would clean this up nicely.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Use destructuring to reduce repetition. Stop when it starts hurting readability.

items.text: Are `async/await` patterns used instead of raw `.then()` chains?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Prefer `async/await` over `.then()` chains for asynchronous code. It reads top-to-bottom like synchronous code.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Promise chains get hard to follow with nesting, error handling, and value passing. `async/await` flattens the control flow and makes errors easier to catch with try/catch.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check for `.then()` chains that could be simplified with `async/await`. Exception: single-line `.then()` for simple transforms is fine.
baseContent.exampleComment: This `.then()` chain is getting complex. Rewriting with `async/await` would make the flow clearer and error handling more straightforward.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: async/await reads like synchronous code. Use it unless you have a specific reason for raw promises.

items.text: Are `Promise.all()` / `Promise.allSettled()` used for concurrent async operations?

baseContent.whatItMeans: When multiple async operations are independent, run them concurrently with `Promise.all()` instead of awaiting them sequentially.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Sequential awaits for independent operations waste time. If three 200ms fetches run in parallel, total time is 200ms instead of 600ms.
baseContent.howToVerify: Look for consecutive `await` calls where the second doesn't depend on the first. Could they run concurrently?
baseContent.exampleComment: These two fetches are independent — `fetchUser` doesn't need `fetchOrders` to complete first. Using `Promise.all([fetchUser(id), fetchOrders(id)])` would cut the wait time in half.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Independent async operations should run concurrently. Sequential awaits are a performance smell.

items.text: Are unhandled promise rejections avoided (missing `catch`, missing `await`)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Every promise must have its rejection handled. Missing `await`, missing `.catch()`, or unhandled rejections in event handlers are common sources of silent failures.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Unhandled rejections crash Node.js processes (since v15) and create silent failures in browsers. They're the async equivalent of uncaught exceptions.
baseContent.howToVerify: Look for promises not being awaited, `.then()` without `.catch()`, and async event handlers without try/catch.
baseContent.exampleComment: This async function is called without `await` in the event handler — if it rejects, the error will be silently swallowed. Add `await` or a `.catch()` handler.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Every promise needs a rejection handler. Silent failures are worse than loud errors.

items.text: Are `for...of` loops used instead of `for...in` for arrays?

baseContent.whatItMeans: `for...in` iterates over object keys (including inherited ones). `for...of` iterates over iterable values. For arrays, always use `for...of`.
baseContent.whyItMatters: `for...in` on arrays iterates string indices, includes prototype properties, and doesn't guarantee order. It's a source of subtle bugs.
baseContent.howToVerify: Search for `for...in` in the diff. Is it being used on an array? Switch to `for...of` or an array method.
baseContent.exampleComment: Using `for...in` on an array iterates string indices and may include prototype properties. Switch to `for...of` for the values.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: for...of for arrays, for...in for object keys. Never mix them up.

items.text: Are array methods (`map`, `filter`, `reduce`) used where clearer than loops?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Functional array methods (`map`, `filter`, `find`, `some`, `every`) express intent more clearly than imperative loops for common transformations.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Array methods communicate what you're doing (transforming, filtering, searching) at a glance. Loops require reading the body to understand the intent.
baseContent.howToVerify: Look for `for` loops that build a new array, filter items, or search for a value. Could an array method express the same thing more clearly?
baseContent.exampleComment: This for loop is building a filtered array. `items.filter(item => item.active)` would express the intent more clearly.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good
codeExamples.code: const active = items.filter(item => item.active);

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Use array methods when they make intent clearer. Use loops when they're simpler.

items.text: Is `reduce` avoided when it hurts readability (prefer `map` + `filter`)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: `reduce` is powerful but often harder to read than a combination of `map`, `filter`, and simple loops. Don't use it just because you can.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Complex `reduce` calls are a common source of review slowdowns. If the reviewer has to mentally simulate the accumulator to understand what's happening, it's too complex.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check any `reduce` calls in the diff. Could the same result be achieved with `map` + `filter`, `Object.fromEntries`, or a simple loop?
baseContent.exampleComment: This reduce is doing a filter + map in one pass. Splitting into `.filter(...).map(...)` would be more readable, and the performance difference is negligible.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: reduce is a code smell when simpler array methods would work. Readability beats cleverness.

items.text: Are nullish coalescing (`??`) and optional chaining (`?.`) used instead of `||` and `&&` checks?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Use `??` (nullish coalescing) and `?.` (optional chaining) instead of `||` and `&&` chains for null/undefined checks.
baseContent.whyItMatters: `||` treats `0`, `''`, and `false` as falsy. `??` only triggers on `null`/`undefined`. Using `||` for defaults causes bugs when valid values are falsy.
baseContent.howToVerify: Look for `||` used as a default value operator. Would `0` or `''` be valid values? If so, `??` is needed. Check `&&` chains that could use `?.`.
baseContent.exampleComment: Using `||` here means a port of `0` would be replaced with the default. Use `??` to only default on null/undefined.

codeExamples.label: Bad
codeExamples.code:
const port = config.port || 3000; // 0 becomes 3000!
const name = user && user.profile && user.profile.name;

codeExamples.label: Good
codeExamples.code:
const port = config.port ?? 3000; // 0 stays 0
const name = user?.profile?.name;

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Use ?? for defaults (null/undefined only) and ?. for safe property access. Reserve || for boolean logic.

items.text: Are `Map` and `Set` used instead of plain objects where semantically appropriate?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Use `Map` for key-value collections and `Set` for unique values instead of plain objects and arrays when the semantics fit better.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Map preserves insertion order, supports any key type, and has better performance for frequent add/delete. Set provides O(1) lookups for uniqueness checks.
baseContent.howToVerify: Look for plain objects used as key-value stores (especially with non-string keys) or arrays used to track uniqueness with `includes()` checks.
baseContent.exampleComment: This array with `.includes()` for dedup checks is O(n) per lookup. A `Set` would give O(1) lookups and make the intent clearer.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Use Set for uniqueness checks and Map for dynamic key-value stores. Plain objects are for fixed structures.

items.text: Are closures and variable scoping correct (no stale closure bugs)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Closures capture variables by reference. In React and async code, this can lead to 'stale closure' bugs where a function uses an outdated value.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Stale closures cause some of the hardest-to-debug issues in React — effects that read old state, event handlers that fire with outdated values, setTimeout callbacks with wrong data.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check useEffect/useCallback/setTimeout for references to state or props. Are they in the dependency array? Could a ref be used for values that shouldn't trigger re-runs?
baseContent.exampleComment: This effect closes over `count` but doesn't include it in the dependency array. It will always see the initial value. Add `count` to deps or use a ref.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Stale closures are React's most common async bug. Use refs for values that change but shouldn't trigger re-runs.

items.text: Are there unit tests for new utility functions and hooks?

baseContent.whatItMeans: New utility functions, hooks, and business logic should have unit tests that verify their behavior.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Untested code is legacy code from the moment it's merged. Tests document behavior, catch regressions, and give confidence for future changes.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check if new functions and hooks have corresponding test files. Are the tests covering the important behaviors and edge cases?
baseContent.exampleComment: This new `formatCurrency()` utility doesn't have tests. It handles multiple currencies and edge cases — tests would catch regressions in this logic.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: New logic needs tests. The PR is when the context is freshest — don't plan to add them later.

items.text: Are tests testing behavior, not implementation?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Tests should verify what the code does (outputs, side effects), not how it does it (internal state, private methods, implementation details).
baseContent.whyItMatters: Implementation-coupled tests break on every refactor even when behavior is unchanged. Behavior-focused tests only break when something actually breaks.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check if tests assert on outputs and visible behavior, or if they check internal state, spy on private methods, or test implementation details.
baseContent.exampleComment: This test checks that `setState` was called with specific arguments. That's testing implementation. Test the visible output instead — what does the user see?

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Test what, not how. Your tests should survive refactoring.

items.text: Are edge cases covered (empty arrays, null, undefined, boundary values)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Tests should include edge cases: empty inputs, null/undefined, zero, negative numbers, boundary values, and single-element collections.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Bugs hide at the edges. A function that works for [1,2,3] might crash on [], return wrong results for [1], or overflow on MAX_SAFE_INTEGER.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check test cases. Are edge cases covered? What happens with empty input? null? Boundary values?
baseContent.exampleComment: The tests cover the happy path but not edge cases. What happens when `items` is empty? When `amount` is 0 or negative? Those are likely to cause bugs.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Always test the edges: empty, one, many, zero, negative, boundary. That's where bugs live.

items.text: Are test names descriptive (`it("should show error when email is invalid")`)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Test names should describe the expected behavior in plain English, forming a readable specification when listed.
baseContent.whyItMatters: When a test fails, the name is your first clue. 'test 1 failed' tells you nothing. 'should return 404 when user not found' tells you exactly what broke.
baseContent.howToVerify: Read the test names. Do they describe behavior? Could someone unfamiliar with the code understand what's being tested from the name alone?
baseContent.exampleComment: This test is named 'works correctly'. What does 'correctly' mean? Something like 'should return sorted results when sort=asc' would be much clearer.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Test names are documentation. Write them so a failure message tells you exactly what broke.

items.text: Are mocks scoped to the test and cleaned up (`jest.restoreAllMocks()`)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Mocks should be created within individual tests or in beforeEach, and cleaned up in afterEach to prevent leaking between tests.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Leaked mocks cause test pollution — a mock from one test affects another, creating flaky tests that pass or fail depending on execution order.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check mock setup. Are mocks created globally or per-test? Is `jest.restoreAllMocks()` called in afterEach? Are spies cleaned up?
baseContent.exampleComment: This mock is created at the module level and never restored. It will leak into every subsequent test. Move it to beforeEach with restoreAllMocks in afterEach.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Scope mocks to tests, restore in afterEach. Global mocks are a flaky test factory.

items.text: Are tests independent (no reliance on execution order)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Each test should pass regardless of which other tests run before it or in what order. Tests should not depend on shared mutable state.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Order-dependent tests cause mysterious CI failures when tests run in parallel or in a different order. They're extremely time-consuming to debug.
baseContent.howToVerify: Could you run any single test in isolation and have it pass? Does any test modify global state without restoring it?
baseContent.exampleComment: This test assumes a user was created by a previous test. If tests run in parallel or in a different order, it fails. Give each test its own setup.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Every test must stand alone. If it can't pass in isolation, it's a flaky test waiting to happen.

items.text: Are test utilities and custom render functions used to reduce boilerplate?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Extract common test setup (rendering with providers, creating mock data, setting up MSW handlers) into shared utilities.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Repeated setup code makes tests verbose and hard to maintain. When the setup changes (new required provider), you update one utility instead of 50 tests.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check for repeated test setup patterns. Are providers wrapped in a custom render? Are mock data factories used?
baseContent.exampleComment: Every test file wraps the component in ThemeProvider, AuthProvider, and Router. A custom `renderWithProviders()` utility would simplify all of these.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Extract shared test setup into utilities. One change to update them all.

items.text: Are `beforeEach`/`afterEach` used for setup/teardown, not repeated in each test?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Common setup and teardown logic should be in beforeEach/afterEach hooks, not copy-pasted into each test.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Duplicated setup is error-prone and tedious to maintain. beforeEach ensures consistent setup, afterEach ensures clean teardown for every test.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check for repeated setup code at the beginning of multiple tests. Could it be in beforeEach? Is teardown handled in afterEach?
baseContent.exampleComment: The same 5 lines of setup appear in every test. Moving them to beforeEach reduces duplication and ensures consistency.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: beforeEach for setup, afterEach for teardown. Don't repeat yourself in tests.

items.text: Is test coverage adequate for the change (not chasing 100%, but covering important paths)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Test coverage should focus on important logic paths, edge cases, and error handling — not on hitting an arbitrary percentage target.
baseContent.whyItMatters: 100% coverage doesn't mean bug-free — it just means every line was executed. Meaningful coverage tests the paths that matter most, especially error paths and edge cases.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check test coverage for the changed files. Are the important logic branches covered? Are error paths tested? Are edge cases handled?
baseContent.exampleComment: This complex calculation function has tests for the happy path but not for the edge cases (division by zero, negative values). Those are the paths most likely to have bugs.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Cover the important paths thoroughly rather than every path superficially. Quality over quantity.

items.text: Are new dependencies justified and vetted (maintained, small, secure)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Before adding a new dependency, evaluate: Is it actively maintained? How large is it? Does it have known vulnerabilities? Could you write this in 20 lines?
baseContent.whyItMatters: Every dependency is a liability — it can have security vulnerabilities, stop being maintained, or add significant bundle size. Vet before you add.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check new entries in package.json. Are they justified? Check npm for maintenance status, bundle size on bundlephobia, and known vulnerabilities.
baseContent.exampleComment: This adds `left-pad` as a dependency for a 3-line function. Consider implementing it inline instead of adding a dependency.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Every dependency has a cost. Justify it against its alternatives including writing it yourself.

items.text: Are dependency versions pinned or using lockfiles (`package-lock.json`, `yarn.lock`)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Use lockfiles (package-lock.json, yarn.lock) to ensure everyone installs the exact same dependency versions. Commit the lockfile.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Without lockfiles, `npm install` on different machines or CI can install different versions, causing 'works on my machine' bugs.
baseContent.howToVerify: Is the lockfile committed and updated? Are there conflicting lockfiles (both package-lock.json and yarn.lock)?
baseContent.exampleComment: The lockfile isn't committed. This means CI and other developers may install different dependency versions. Please commit the lockfile.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Commit your lockfile. It's the source of truth for dependency versions.

items.text: Are peer dependency warnings resolved?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Peer dependency warnings indicate version conflicts between packages. They should be resolved, not ignored.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Unresolved peer dependencies cause runtime errors when packages expect different versions of shared dependencies.
baseContent.howToVerify: Run `npm install` and check for peer dependency warnings. Are they addressed?
baseContent.exampleComment: There's a peer dependency warning: react-query expects React 18 but we're on React 17. This may cause runtime issues. Let's resolve the version conflict.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Don't ignore peer dependency warnings. They're telling you about real version conflicts.

items.text: Are circular dependencies avoided?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Module A imports B which imports A. This creates circular dependencies that can cause undefined imports, initialization order bugs, and bundling issues.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Circular dependencies create initialization race conditions — you get `undefined` instead of the expected export. They're hard to debug and indicate poor architecture.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check import chains. Does the new file import from a module that already imports from it? Tools like `madge` can visualize the dependency graph.
baseContent.exampleComment: This creates a circular dependency: userService imports from authService which imports from userService. Extract shared logic to a third module to break the cycle.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Break circular dependencies by extracting shared logic into a third module.

items.text: Are barrel files (`index.ts`) not causing excessive bundling?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Barrel files (`index.ts` that re-exports everything) can cause the bundler to include more code than needed because importing one thing pulls in everything.
baseContent.whyItMatters: A barrel file that re-exports 50 components means importing one component may bundle all 50. This defeats code splitting and tree shaking.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check if imports go through barrel files. Does the bundler tree-shake correctly? Could direct file imports be used instead?
baseContent.exampleComment: Importing from the barrel file pulls in all 30 components. Importing directly from `./UserCard/UserCard` would only bundle what's needed.

codeExamples.label: Bad
codeExamples.code: import { UserCard } from '@/components'; // barrel file imports all 30 components

codeExamples.label: Good
codeExamples.code: import { UserCard } from '@/components/UserCard'; // only imports what's needed

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Barrel files are convenient but can bloat bundles. Use direct imports for large module collections.

items.text: Are imports organized consistently (external, internal, relative)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Imports should follow a consistent order: external packages first, then internal modules, then relative imports. Separated by blank lines.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Consistent import ordering makes files easier to scan and reduces merge conflicts from import shuffling.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check import order. Does it follow the project convention? Are external and internal imports grouped?
baseContent.exampleComment: These imports mix external packages and local files. Grouping them (externals first, then internals, then relative) would be easier to scan.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: External → internal → relative. Consistent import order reduces noise in diffs.

items.text: Are unused dependencies removed?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Dependencies listed in package.json but not imported anywhere should be removed. They add install time, potential vulnerabilities, and confusion.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Unused dependencies slow down installs, increase attack surface (each package is a potential vulnerability), and confuse developers about what the project actually uses.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check if removed imports leave behind unused package.json entries. Tools like `depcheck` can find unused dependencies.
baseContent.exampleComment: The `moment` package is still in package.json but was replaced with `date-fns` in this PR. Let's remove it to keep dependencies clean.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: If you're not importing it, remove it from package.json. Dead dependencies are dead weight.

items.text: Are dev dependencies vs production dependencies classified correctly?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Testing, linting, and build tools belong in `devDependencies`. Only packages needed at runtime belong in `dependencies`.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Dev dependencies in `dependencies` bloat production installs and Docker images. Runtime packages in `devDependencies` cause production crashes.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check new package.json entries. Are test tools in devDependencies? Are runtime needs in dependencies?
baseContent.exampleComment: Jest is in `dependencies` instead of `devDependencies`. It's only needed for testing, so it shouldn't ship to production.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: devDependencies for build/test tools, dependencies for runtime needs. Misclassification has real consequences.

items.text: Are REST conventions followed (resource nouns, HTTP verbs, proper status codes)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: RESTful APIs use resource nouns as URLs (`/users`, `/orders`) and HTTP methods for actions (GET to read, POST to create, PUT/PATCH to update, DELETE to remove).
baseContent.whyItMatters: Consistent REST conventions make APIs predictable. Developers can guess endpoints without reading docs. Non-standard patterns create confusion.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check URL patterns. Are they resource-based nouns? Do HTTP methods match the operation? Are status codes correct?
baseContent.exampleComment: This endpoint uses `POST /getUser` instead of `GET /users/:id`. Following REST conventions would make the API more predictable.

codeExamples.label: Bad
codeExamples.code:
router.post('/getUsers', handler); // verb in URL, wrong method
router.post('/deleteUser', handler); // should be DELETE

codeExamples.label: Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Nouns for URLs, verbs for HTTP methods. REST conventions exist so APIs don't need to reinvent naming.

items.text: Are request/response schemas versioned or backward compatible?

baseContent.whatItMeans: API changes should be backward compatible. If breaking changes are needed, use versioning (v1/v2) so existing clients continue working.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Breaking API changes without versioning crash existing clients. Mobile apps can't be force-updated, so old versions must keep working.
baseContent.howToVerify: Does this PR change API response shapes? Are existing fields being removed or renamed? Is there a migration path for clients?
baseContent.exampleComment: Renaming `user_name` to `username` in the response will break existing clients. Consider adding `username` alongside `user_name` and deprecating the old field.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Add before you remove. Deprecate before you delete. Version when you must break.

items.text: Are API types shared between frontend and backend (monorepo) or generated from OpenAPI/GraphQL?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Frontend and backend should share the same type definitions for API requests and responses, either via a shared package in a monorepo or generated from OpenAPI/GraphQL schemas.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Without shared types, frontend and backend types drift apart silently. A field rename on the backend doesn't produce a compile error on the frontend.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check if API types are shared between frontend and backend. Are they generated from a schema? Or manually duplicated?
baseContent.exampleComment: The frontend has its own `User` type that doesn't match the backend's response. Sharing types from a common package would catch mismatches at compile time.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Share API types between frontend and backend. One source of truth prevents drift.

items.text: Are pagination, filtering, and sorting implemented for list endpoints?

baseContent.whatItMeans: List endpoints should support pagination (limit/offset or cursor), filtering, and sorting. Never return unbounded result sets.
baseContent.whyItMatters: An endpoint that returns all 100,000 records crashes the server, overloads the client, and is unusable. Pagination is essential for any list that grows.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check list endpoints. Do they accept pagination parameters? Is there a maximum page size? What happens with no filters?
baseContent.exampleComment: This endpoint returns all orders with no pagination. When there are 50,000 orders, this will timeout. Add cursor-based pagination with a default limit.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Paginate every list endpoint. Default limits, maximum limits, and cursor-based navigation.

items.text: Are N+1 query patterns avoided in GraphQL resolvers (use DataLoader)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: N+1 queries happen when resolving a list: 1 query for the list, then N queries for each item's related data. Use DataLoader to batch these.
baseContent.whyItMatters: A list of 100 items with N+1 queries makes 101 database calls instead of 2. This causes slow responses and database overload.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check GraphQL resolvers for field resolvers that make individual DB calls. Could they be batched with DataLoader?
baseContent.exampleComment: This resolver queries the database for each user's posts individually — 100 users = 100 queries. Use DataLoader to batch these into a single query.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Use DataLoader in GraphQL resolvers. It batches N individual queries into 1 batch query.

items.text: Is data normalized on the client or server as appropriate?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Data should be normalized (flat, referenced by ID) rather than deeply nested. The normalization can happen server-side (API response) or client-side (state management).
baseContent.whyItMatters: Nested data duplicates entities and makes updates error-prone. If a user's name changes, you'd have to update it everywhere it appears. Normalized data updates in one place.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check data structures in state. Are entities duplicated across different parts of the state? Would normalizing (by ID) simplify updates?
baseContent.exampleComment: Each order contains a full copy of the user object. If we normalize and store users separately (by ID), updating a user's name propagates everywhere automatically.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Normalize data to avoid duplication. One source of truth per entity.

items.text: Is user input validated on both client and server?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Client-side validation provides UX (instant feedback). Server-side validation provides security (can't be bypassed). You need both.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Client-side validation can be bypassed with browser devtools or API calls. Server-side validation without client feedback is a poor UX. Both are needed.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check new forms. Is there client-side validation for UX? Is the same validation enforced server-side? Can the server validation be bypassed from the client?
baseContent.exampleComment: This form has client-side email validation but the server accepts any string. An attacker can bypass the client validation with a direct API call.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Client validation for UX, server validation for security. Never rely on just one.

items.text: Are XSS vectors mitigated (no `dangerouslySetInnerHTML` with user content)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Cross-site scripting (XSS) occurs when user-provided content is rendered as HTML. React auto-escapes JSX, but dangerouslySetInnerHTML bypasses this.
baseContent.whyItMatters: XSS attacks can steal session tokens, redirect users to malicious sites, and execute arbitrary code in the user's browser.
baseContent.howToVerify: Search for `dangerouslySetInnerHTML` with user content, `innerHTML` assignments, and any place where user input becomes HTML.
baseContent.exampleComment: This renders user comments with dangerouslySetInnerHTML. An attacker could inject `<script>` tags. Use a markdown renderer or sanitize with DOMPurify.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Never render user content as raw HTML. React's JSX escaping is your friend — don't bypass it.

items.text: Are CSRF protections in place for state-changing requests?

baseContent.whatItMeans: CSRF (Cross-Site Request Forgery) tricks authenticated users into making unwanted requests. Protect state-changing endpoints with CSRF tokens or SameSite cookies.
baseContent.whyItMatters: A malicious site can submit a form to your API using the user's browser cookies, performing actions as the authenticated user without their knowledge.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check state-changing endpoints. Are CSRF tokens validated? Are cookies set with SameSite=Strict/Lax? Does the API check Origin/Referer headers?
baseContent.exampleComment: This form submits to the API without a CSRF token. If cookies are used for auth, a malicious site could forge this request. Add CSRF protection.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: CSRF tokens for form submissions, SameSite cookies, or both. Stateful APIs need CSRF protection.

items.text: Are authentication and authorization checked for every protected route (both frontend guards and backend middleware)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Every protected route must verify both who the user is (authentication) and what they can do (authorization). Both frontend guards and backend middleware are needed.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Frontend-only auth can be bypassed. Missing authorization means any authenticated user can access any resource. Both layers are required.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check new protected routes. Is auth middleware applied? Are authorization checks (user can access this resource) in place? Can the frontend guard be bypassed?
baseContent.exampleComment: This admin endpoint checks authentication but not authorization. Any logged-in user can access admin functions. Add a role check middleware.

codeExamples.label: Bad
codeExamples.code:
// Only checks if logged in, not if authorized
router.delete('/users/:id', authMiddleware, deleteUser);

codeExamples.label: Good
codeExamples.code:
// Checks both authentication and authorization
router.delete('/users/:id', authMiddleware, requireRole('admin'), deleteUser);

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Authentication (who are you?) + Authorization (can you do this?). Both are required.

items.text: Are secrets absent from client-side code and git history?

baseContent.whatItMeans: API keys, tokens, and secrets must never appear in frontend code (including env vars bundled by webpack/vite) or in git history.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Client-side code is visible to everyone. Secrets in git history persist even after deletion. Once exposed, keys must be rotated.
baseContent.howToVerify: Search the diff for strings that look like secrets. Check .env files — are NEXT_PUBLIC_/VITE_ prefixed vars actually safe to be public?
baseContent.exampleComment: This `NEXT_PUBLIC_API_SECRET` env var is exposed in client code. The `NEXT_PUBLIC_` prefix means it's bundled into the frontend. Secrets must never have this prefix.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: If it's a secret, it can't be in client code. NEXT_PUBLIC_ and VITE_ vars are public.

items.text: Are third-party scripts evaluated for security (CDN integrity hashes)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Third-party scripts loaded from CDNs should have Subresource Integrity (SRI) hashes to ensure they haven't been tampered with.
baseContent.whyItMatters: A compromised CDN could serve malicious JavaScript. SRI hashes ensure the browser rejects modified scripts, protecting against supply chain attacks.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check for script tags loading from external CDNs. Do they have integrity attributes? Is the CDN trustworthy?
baseContent.exampleComment: This script loads from a CDN without an integrity hash. If the CDN is compromised, malicious code would run on your site. Add an SRI hash.

codeExamples.label: Bad
codeExamples.code: <script src="https://cdn.example.com/lib.js"></script>

codeExamples.label: Good
codeExamples.code:
<script
  src="https://cdn.example.com/lib.js"
  integrity="sha384-abc123..."
  crossorigin="anonymous"
></script>

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Use SRI hashes for CDN scripts. They're your protection against CDN compromise.

items.text: Are Content Security Policy headers configured?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Content Security Policy (CSP) headers restrict what resources the browser can load, preventing XSS and data injection attacks.
baseContent.whyItMatters: CSP is a defense-in-depth layer against XSS. Even if an attacker injects a script, CSP can prevent it from loading external resources or executing inline code.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check if CSP headers are configured. Are they restrictive enough? Do they allow `unsafe-inline` or `unsafe-eval` (weakens protection)?
baseContent.exampleComment: No CSP headers are configured. Adding a Content-Security-Policy header that restricts script sources would provide XSS defense-in-depth.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: CSP is your last line of defense against XSS. Start restrictive, loosen only where needed.

items.text: Are file paths sanitized to prevent directory traversal?

baseContent.whatItMeans: User-provided file paths must be sanitized to prevent `../../../etc/passwd` style directory traversal attacks.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Directory traversal lets attackers read or write files outside the intended directory, potentially accessing system files, config files, or other users' data.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check if user input is used in file paths (fs.readFile, path.join). Is the resulting path validated to be within the expected directory?
baseContent.exampleComment: This uses `path.join(uploadDir, req.params.filename)` without validating the result. A filename like `../../etc/passwd` would escape the upload directory.

codeExamples.label: Bad
codeExamples.code:
const filePath = path.join(UPLOAD_DIR, req.params.filename);
const content = fs.readFileSync(filePath); // traversal possible

codeExamples.label: Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Use path.basename() and verify the resolved path stays within bounds. Never trust user-provided paths.

items.text: Are JSDoc comments added for exported functions and complex logic?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Exported functions and complex logic should have JSDoc comments explaining what they do, their parameters, and return values.
baseContent.whyItMatters: JSDoc powers IDE tooltips, enabling developers to understand functions without reading the implementation. It's documentation that lives with the code.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check new exported functions. Do they have JSDoc with @param and @returns? Is complex logic explained?
baseContent.exampleComment: This exported utility function doesn't have JSDoc. Adding a brief description and @param/@returns would help consumers understand it without reading the source.

codeExamples.label: Good
codeExamples.code:
/**
 * Formats a price in the user's locale with the correct currency symbol.
 * @param amount - Price in cents
 * @param currency - ISO 4217 currency code (default: 'USD')
 * @returns Formatted price string (e.g., '$12.99')
 */
export function formatPrice(amount: number, currency = 'USD'): string {

baseContent.keyTakeaway: JSDoc on exports is documentation with IDE superpowers. A sentence of context saves minutes of reading.

items.text: Are README files updated for new setup steps or environment variables?

baseContent.whatItMeans: When a PR adds new setup steps, environment variables, or dependencies, the README should be updated so new developers can get started.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Outdated setup docs waste hours of new developer time. If the README says `npm start` but the PR adds a required Redis dependency, new devs are stuck.
baseContent.howToVerify: Does this PR add new env vars, setup steps, or infrastructure dependencies? Is the README updated to reflect them?
baseContent.exampleComment: This PR adds a Redis dependency but the README doesn't mention it. A new developer following the setup guide would get a confusing connection error.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: If your PR changes how to set up or run the project, update the README.

items.text: Are API endpoints documented (OpenAPI/Swagger, GraphQL schema)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: API endpoints should be documented with OpenAPI/Swagger for REST or schema documentation for GraphQL, so consumers know how to use them.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Undocumented APIs force consumers to read source code or reverse-engineer the API. Documentation enables self-service and reduces support burden.
baseContent.howToVerify: Are new endpoints added to the API docs (OpenAPI spec, Swagger, GraphQL schema docs)? Can a consumer find and understand the new endpoint?
baseContent.exampleComment: This new endpoint isn't documented in the OpenAPI spec. Frontend developers will have to read the source code to know the request/response shape.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Document your APIs. Undocumented endpoints are unusable without reading source code.

items.text: Are breaking changes called out in the PR description?

baseContent.whatItMeans: If the PR introduces breaking changes (renamed fields, removed endpoints, changed behavior), these must be explicitly called out in the PR description.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Breaking changes that aren't called out get merged without downstream teams preparing. This causes outages when the change reaches production.
baseContent.howToVerify: Does this PR change API contracts, remove features, or alter existing behavior? Is this documented in the PR description?
baseContent.exampleComment: This renames the `userId` field to `user_id` in the API response — that's a breaking change for all clients. Please call this out prominently in the PR description.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Breaking changes need a spotlight, not a footnote. Call them out clearly.

items.text: Are Storybook stories added for new UI components?

baseContent.whatItMeans: New UI components should have Storybook stories showing their various states (default, loading, error, disabled, edge cases).
baseContent.whyItMatters: Storybook serves as a living style guide and visual regression testing tool. Stories document component states that are hard to reach in the full app.
baseContent.howToVerify: Does the new component have a Storybook story? Does it show the important states (default, loading, error, empty)?
baseContent.exampleComment: This new Button component doesn't have a Storybook story. Adding stories for default, disabled, and loading states would document its API and enable visual testing.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Storybook stories are component documentation that's always up to date. Add them for new UI components.
